segments.s297-g257: بسوزم is a first-person optative/subjunctive (بـ- + سوختن) meaning “may I burn” or “let me burn,” used here as a lamenting wish. بر او is a prepositional phrase (بر + pronoun) marking the object/target of that grief (“because of/for him”). تیره is an adjective placed before جان and means “dark/ill‑fated,” modifying جان (life, soul); پدرش is a genitival phrase “his father” where ش is the possessive suffix attached to پدر.
segments.s297-g258: چو here is the conjunction “when/as,” introducing a circumstantial clause. کاووس را uses را as the definite/object marker (کاووس marked as the affected entity) and is placed before the verb for emphasis; سوخت is the past verb “burned” (used metaphorically = grieved/burned with sorrow). او after the verb is the subject pronoun (classical order often places verb before subject), and بر پسرش is a prepositional phrase “for/over his son,” with ش as the possessive suffix on پسر.
segments.s297-g259: دلیران و شیران forms a coordinated noun phrase “the brave ones and the lions,” where شیران is figurative (“lions” = brave men) rather than literal beasts. ایران‌زمین is a postpositive compound meaning “the land of Iran” and specifies the domain to which these brave figures belong.
segments.s297-g260: همه is the quantifier “all/everyone.” شاه را uses را as the definite object marker indicating the king is the target of the action. خواندند is the 3rd‑person plural past “they called” or “they cried (out),” and آفرین is an interjection “Bravo”/acclamation that they shouted to the king.
segments.s297-g201: همی before the verb marks a continuous or habitual action ('kept/was continually'). لب به دندان is an idiomatic prepositional phrase ('lip to tooth') meaning he clamped or held his lip with his teeth; the verb بخایید is an archaic poetic form (from خاییدن) describing that held/external expression. شاه is the subject placed after the verb phrase, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s297-g202: همی again marks ongoing action. کرد here functions as a light verb 'to make/do' that combines with the noun نگاه to form 'to cast/bring a look'; خیره is a modifier meaning 'staring/fixed' and بدیشان is the prepositional object 'at them'. Word order places the subject (شاه) earlier or implied from the previous clause.
segments.s297-g203: وز آن پس is an archaic phrase meaning 'after that' (وز here functions like classical از). چنین گفت is 'thus he said' (گفت is simple past 'said' in narrative). کاین نیست راه is a verbless nominal clause introduced by ک (short for 'که این') meaning 'that this is not a way' — راه is the predicate.
segments.s297-g204: به ایران خرامیم is a 1st-person-plural present/habitual verb (خرامیم 'we stride/proudly go') with the goal expressed by به ایران ('to Iran'). ز این رزمگاه is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from this battlefield' indicating origin; z (ز) functions as 'from'. The phrase as a whole gives the route/source relation for the motion verb.
segments.s297-g205: کجا is a rhetorical interrogative 'where (are)'. آن همه is a strong quantifier 'all those' placed before the nouns; رسم و سوگند ما is a noun phrase 'our customs and oaths' with the possessive pronoun ما following the nouns, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s297-g206: همان here means 'that very / the same' and introduces a parallel list; بدره is a poetic term ('beauty' or 'radiance') rather than literally 'full moon' in this context, and is coordinated with گفته ('words/what was said') and پند ('advice,counsel'). ما at the end governs the whole coordinated phrase ('our ...').
segments.s297-g207: چو here introduces a conditional/temporal clause ('if/when'). بر تخت is the locative prepositional phrase 'on the throne'. بر زنده افراسیاب is a poetic stacking where زنده ('alive') modifies the proper name افراسیاب; the subject is effectively 'Afrasiab (being) alive on the throne.'
segments.s297-g208: بماند is a present/subjunctive-like form 'remains / should remain' introducing the result; جهان گردد uses گردد (from گشت) as a subjunctive/future-like 'will become'. از وی خراب is a causal phrase 'ruined because of him' where از وی gives the agent/cause and خراب is the predicate adjective 'ruined/destroyed'.
segments.s297-g249: همان اسبش (that very horse of his) places the demonstrative همان before the possessed noun اسبش (اسب + enclitic -ش). از باد دارد نژاد is a stative phrase: دارد (present 'has/possesses') takes the noun نژاد (lineage, breed) and the source is marked by از باد ('from the wind'), so literally 'has lineage from the wind.' This is a poetic expression of descent.
segments.s297-g250: به دل همچو شیر is a predicative phrase meaning 'in (its) heart like a lion' where به + noun (دل) marks the sphere/state and همچو introduces the simile. و links a second simile: به رفتن چو باد uses the infinitive/masdar رفتن with به to indicate manner ('in its going/running') and چو باد = 'like the wind' (چو = like). Together they describe courage (heart) and manner of movement (speed).
segments.s297-g251: کسی را که...فر is a relative clause: کسی را (someone; را marks the person as definite/object of the relative) که introduces the clause یزدان ندادست فر — a past-tense verb phrase 'God did not give farr.' ندادست is an archaic/localized past form meaning 'did not give.' The whole segment = 'someone to whom God has not given farr.'
segments.s297-g252: نباشدش is a negated present/stative form with the clitic ش (for him/her): 'he has not / it is not for him.' با چنگ او پای و پر lists means/equipment ('with his claw'—چنگ as talon/claw) and objects ('foot and feather') that he will lack; word order is emphatic/poetic (negation + clitic before the instrumental phrase). The phrase says that even with his 'claw' he will have no 'foot or wing' (i.e. no power/status).
segments.s297-g253: همان با شما او نیاید به جنگ places emphasis on همان and uses the clitic order (همان ... او) with نیاید (negative present/subjunctive 'does not / will not come') — 'that one will not come to battle with you.' The prepositional phrase به جنگ marks the destination/occasion (into battle).
segments.s297-g254: ز فر و نژاد خود آیدش ننگ is a clause where ننگ (shame) is the noun and آیدش ('comes to him') is the verb with the clitic ش. ز marks source or cause ('from his farr and lineage'), and خود intensifies 'his own.' The literal order 'from [his] farr and lineage comes to him shame' means 'his farr and lineage will bring him shame.'
segments.s297-g255: نبیره فریدون و پور قباد lists two epithets/lineages: نبیره فریدون = 'a descendant/scion of Fereydun' and پور قباد = 'the son/offspring of Qobad.' These are name phrases formed by a kin-term + proper name, typical of epic genealogy.
segments.s297-g256: دو جنگی بود is 'there were two warriors' (جنگی as 'warlike/fighter' and بود past 'were'), followed by the coordinated description یک‌دل و یک نهاد — 'of one heart and one nature/disposition.' This is a classical idiom meaning united in purpose; note the adjective compounds follow the copula for emphasis.
segments.s297-g193: هر آن شهر = "every/each city" (هر + demonstrative آن). کز introduces a relative clause (archaic for که + از) meaning "which/from"; مرز ایران is the noun phrase "the border of Iran." نهی is an archaic predicate here meaning "lies beyond/is distant from" (classical usage), so the whole segment is a relative clause: "every city which is beyond the border of Iran."
segments.s297-g194: همی is the habitual/continuous adverb (marks repeated or customary action). کرد is the main verb "did/made" used here in narrative; خواهد follows as a modal/future-in-the-past element giving a sense of "would" or "would cause to be." ز + ترکان + تهی is a prepositional phrase meaning "empty of Turks," with ز used poetically for "of/from" and تهی an adjective "devoid/empty (of)."
segments.s297-g195: به ایران is a directional prepositional phrase "into/toward Iran." خرامیم is 1st person plural present/habitual "we stride/sashay" (خرامیدن). پیروز و شاد are predicate adjectives joined by و describing the subject as "victorious and glad." Word order places the directional phrase first for emphasis.
segments.s297-g196: ز کار گذشته = "from/of past deeds" (ز = poetic "from/of"). نگیریم is 1st person plural present/subjunctive negative of گرفتن (here "we do not take/recall"), with the object یاد "remembrance/memory" placed after the verb (classical word order permits this). The full sense: "we do not recall the deeds that are past."
segments.s297-g197: بر این = "at/on this" (i.e., at these words or regarding this). گفته بودند is a past/perfect construction (گفته + بودند) meaning "had said" or "were saying" in the narrative past; پیر و جوان is the compound subject "the old and the young," placed after the verb for emphasis. Overall: "the old and the young had said this."
segments.s297-g198: جز از = "except for/except of" (جز followed by از). نامور modifies the proper name that follows, giving "the renowned Rostam," and پهلوان is an appositive title "champion/hero." The phrase means "except for the famed champion Rostam."
segments.s297-g199: که introduces the subordinate clause "that/for." رستم is the subject. همی marks habitual or continuous aspect. ز آشتی = "from reconciliation/peace" (ز = from/because of). سر بگاشت is an idiomatic verbal phrase (سر + بگاشت) meaning literally "he lifted/raised his head" and here figuratively "he took a stand/turned away from reconciliation" (classical usage).
segments.s297-g200: ز درد سیاوش = "because of Siavash's suffering/pain" (ز = because/of; درد here carries emotional/affective sense). به دل کینه داشت is a fixed expression: به دل = "in (his) heart," کینه داشت = "he had hatred/a grudge." Together: "he bore a grudge in his heart because of Siavash's suffering."
segments.s297-g97: 'گزین کرد' is a past-tense verbal phrase ('he chose'); the patient/selected group follows in a prepositional phrase 'از موبدان' (from the mobeds) with the numeral and noun 'چار مرد' specifying what was chosen (four men). 'موبدان' is the plural of 'موبد' (Zoroastrian priest).
segments.s297-g98: 'چشیده' is a past participle used adjectivally — 'having tasted/experienced' — and modifies the four men; 'بسی' is an adverb meaning 'much/very' qualifying 'چشیده'; 'از جهان گرم و سرد' is an adverbial phrase 'of/from the world — hot and cold', i.e. pleasures and hardships. Word order places the participial description after the noun it qualifies.
segments.s297-g99: Here 'وز' is a poetic fused form (functioning like 'و از') meaning 'and from'; it introduces the source group. 'آن نامداران' = 'those renowned ones' (demonstrative + plural adjective) and 'لشکر' = 'an army/host' (the numeral 'هزار' that follows in the full phrase specifies its size: 'a thousand-strong army').
segments.s297-g100: The adjectives 'خردمند و شایسته' ('wise and worthy/fit') modify 'لشکر' — they describe the army's qualities; 'کارزار' ('battle') indicates the sphere in which the army is suitable, so the phrase means 'wise and fit for battle.'
segments.s297-g101: 'به ره' is a locative phrase 'on/along the road'; 'چون' here introduces a temporal clause 'when/as'; 'طلایه' is the subject 'the vanguard'; 'بدیدش' is past tense 'he saw it/him' with the enclitic object '-ش' ('him/it'); 'ز دور' is an adverbial phrase 'from afar'. Together: 'When he saw the vanguard on the road from afar...'.
segments.s297-g102: A noun phrase listing what was seen: 'درفش و سنان' = 'the banner and the spears' followed by 'سواران تور' = 'the riders of Tūr' (the Turanian horsemen), where the genitive is expressed by juxtaposition ('سواران تور' = 'Tūr's riders').
segments.s297-g103: 'ز ترکان' = 'from among the Turks'; 'هر آن کس که' is a correlative relative clause 'whoever / anyone who'; 'بد پیشرو' is a nominal phrase 'a bad/ill-suited vanguard' (بد modifies پیشرو). The clause identifies a subset among the Turks.
segments.s297-g104: 'ز ناکاردیده جوانان نو' is a descriptive/genitive phrase that completes the idea: 'was from the untried/inexperienced new youths.' 'ناکاردیده' is an adjective meaning 'not tried / inexperienced'; 'جوانان نو' = 'new young men' (i.e. fresh recruits). The preposition 'ز' ('from') links this phrase to the preceding relative clause (identifying origin).
segments.s297-g225: "که" here is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing reported content. "از جنگ ایشان" is a prepositional/causal phrase 'because of/from their battle/fighting' (ایشان = 'they/them'). "گرفتی" is 2nd person singular past of گرفتن 'to take', used with the noun "شتاب" as its object to mean 'you took (or showed) haste' = 'you hurried'. Word order places the prepositional phrase and object around the verb in a literary way.
segments.s297-g226: "به گفت" functions as an archaic/verbal phrase attributing speech, roughly 'said by' / 'thus said'. "فریبنده" is an epithet 'the deceptive' modifying the name that follows, and "افراسیاب" is the proper name; together: 'said Afrasiab the deceiver.'
segments.s297-g227: "چو" is the archaic conjunction 'when'. The phrase supplies the subject and its object: "ایرانیان" (the Iranians) + "این سخنها" (these words; plural) + "ز شاه" ('from the king' — ز is the poetic form of 'from'). The verb follows in the next segment.
segments.s297-g228: "شنیدند" is 3rd-person plural past 'they heard'. "و" links clauses. "پیچان شدند" is a compound of the adjective/participle "پیچان" ('twisted/disturbed') with the auxiliary/verb "شدند" ('they became'), yielding 'they became disturbed/perturbed'. "از گناه" gives the cause: 'because of (the) blame/sin.'
segments.s297-g229: "گرفتند پوزش" literally 'they took apology' — an idiom meaning 'they offered/asked pardon' or 'they apologized'. "که" introduces their explanatory statement/quote. "ما بنده‌ایم" is the humble self-reference 'we are servants/slaves' (a conventional formula of submission/humility).
segments.s297-g230: "هم" here adds emphasis 'also/even'. "از مهربانی" ('because of/from your kindness') gives the cause of their feeling. "سرافگنده‌ایم" is 1st-person plural present 'we are ashamed/abashed' (سرافگنده + ایم), so the clause means 'we are also abashed because of your kindness.'
segments.s297-g231: "نخواهد" is the (present/future) form of خواستن 'to want' used here as 'desires' (literary). In this verb‑first, subject‑after order the subject "شهنشاه" follows the verb. The construction "نخواهد ... جز ..." means 'wants nothing except ...' so "جز نام نیک" = 'except a good name' (i.e. he desires only a good reputation).
segments.s297-g232: "وگر" is an archaic conditional/contrastive 'and if / otherwise'. "کارها را" is the definite/plural 'the deeds/tasks' with the object marker "را" used topically. "سرانجام نیک" is a noun phrase 'a good outcome/ending' (سرانجام 'result/outcome' modified postpositively by نیک). Together the clause expresses the wish that the deeds have a good ending if anything else is at issue.
segments.s297-g113: به ایرانیان گفت is a prepositional phrase + speech verb: به (to) marks the indirect object 'the Iranians', followed by گفت (said). Word order is typical Persian: indirect object before the reporting verb. Here گفت introduces an instruction addressed to those Iranians.
segments.s297-g114: This clause contains the command: سواری (a rider/horseman) is the direct object, and فرستید is an exhortative/imperative form meaning 'send' (contextually an instruction). با رسم و راه is an adverbial phrase 'with ceremony and manner' (با = 'with', رسم = 'ceremony/protocol', راه = 'way/manner') qualifying how the sending should be done.
segments.s297-g115: بگوید که introduces reported content: بگوید is a reporting verb form used in narrative to introduce what follows (here glossed 'he said/let it be said'), and که introduces the subordinate clause. روشن دلی is a compound adjectival phrase 'bright/clear‑hearted' (روشن qualifying دلی), و شیده نام is an epithet 'called Shideh' (نام following a name/adjective marks 'named/so‑called').
segments.s297-g116: به شاه آوریدست چندی پیام is the rest of the reported clause: به شاه 'to the king' (prepositional phrase), آوریدست is a perfect/resultative form 'has brought' (classical compound perfect), and چندی پیام means 'some/several messages' (چندی quantifies پیام). Together they state that that person brought messages to the king.
segments.s297-g117: از marks origin/source 'from Afrasiab'. آن سپهدار چین is an appositive description: آن 'that/the', سپهدار 'commander', چین here refers to 'Chin/China' (a geographic epithet). The whole group identifies the sender as 'Afrasiab, that commander of Chin.'
segments.s297-g118: This noun group lists people/relations: پدر مادر is an idiomatic pair 'father and mother' (parents), followed by شاه 'the king' and ایران‌زمین 'the land of Iran' as a genitival specification. Grammatically this names whose parents are meant (the parents of the Iranian king).
segments.s297-g119: This clause begins with سواری 'a horseman'. دمان appears immediately after as a name or identifier (here functionally a proper name) — in classical narratives names sometimes follow the noun. از طلایه برفت is a classical phrasing: از طلایه 'from the vanguard/advance guard' and برفت is an older/perfective past of رفت 'he went/left', so the clause means 'a horseman (Doman) left the vanguard.'
segments.s297-g120: بر شاه ایران خرامید is a simple past clause with motion: بر here 'toward/at' + شاه ایران 'the king of Iran' as the goal, and خرامید is a past tense verb 'he strode/proudly paced' (an intransitive motion verb). The final word تفت appears after the verb; in the manuscript it may be an interjection or a name/epithet, but as given its listed dictionary senses don't match the narrative use, so it's left as a terminal element in the line.
segments.s297-g161: همه is a quantifier 'all/everyone' placed before the groups it quantifies. بخردان is a plural agentive/adjectival form 'the wise/prudent ones' and ردان is a parallel coordinator joined by و; سپاه (host/army) names the group they belong to (literally 'all the wise and rādān of the army'). The whole phrase is a quantified noun phrase headed by سپاه with the two classes specified before it.
segments.s297-g162: به آواز is an adverbial phrase ('in/with a voice' → 'aloud'), where به functions adverbially. گفتند is the 3rd-person plural past narrative 'they said' (here used as reported speech). کاین introduces the content of their speech ('that/this'), نیست is the negative copula 'is not', and راه is the predicate noun 'way/method' — together: 'they said aloud that this is not the way.'
segments.s297-g163: جهاندیده and پردانش are adjectival/epithetic compounds placed before the proper name to qualify it: 'worldly-wise' (جهاندیده) and 'learned' (پردانش). They directly modify the following proper name افراسیاب.
segments.s297-g164: جز از ... نبیند is an exceptive construction: جز از + noun/infinitive = 'except (for) ...'. چاره جستن is an infinitival noun phrase 'the seeking of a remedy' (چاره 'remedy/solution' + جستن 'to seek'). نبیند is a classical 3rd-person singular negative form meaning 'he did not see' or 'he saw not'; به خواب ('in sleep'/'in a dream') is an adjunct specifying the context. The whole clause: 'he saw nothing in sleep except seeking a solution.'
segments.s297-g165: نداند is the negative past/archaic present 3rd-person singular of دانستن, here meaning 'he did not know'. جز از introduces the exception list ('except from/of'), and تنبل و جادویی are the first items in that list: تنبل 'laziness/laity (lazy-ness)' (used as a noun/adjective) and جادویی 'magical/sorcerous'. The structure is 'he knew nothing except ...'.
segments.s297-g166: This continues the exception list connected by و (and): فریب 'deception/trick', بداندیشی 'malice/ill-intent', and بدخویی 'ill-temper/unkindness'. Each noun is coordinated; there is no new verb — they all serve as items that he 'knew nothing of' except these.
segments.s297-g167: ز is the poetic form of از 'from', so ز لشکر means 'from the army'. کنون is an adverb 'now' modifying the action. شیده is the noun/adjective of the person chosen ('shideh' = love‑smitten / mad), and را is the definite object marker marking شیده as the direct object. برگزید is the 3rd-person singular past 'he chose/selected'.
segments.s297-g168: که here introduces the explanatory subordinate clause and functions as 'for/because/that'. این دید is 'this sight/vision' (دید used as a noun 'vision'), بند بدی را is a noun phrase 'the bond/fetter of evil' with را marking it as the object of the predicate, and کلید is the predicate noun 'key' (figurative). The clause reads 'for he saw/it was seen that this vision is the key to the bond of evil.'
segments.s297-g49: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (classical form of از). گنج بزرگان is a genitive phrase 'treasure(s) of the great/elders' (noun + plural genitive), and تخت و کلاه are simply coordinated nouns 'throne and (royal) headgear/crown' joined by و. Word order is noun phrase followed by additional items; no finite verb appears in this fragment.
segments.s297-g50: ز 'from' begins another prepositional phrase. چیزی (here 'anything/something') is the indefinite head, followed by a relative clause introduced by که + باید, where باید carries the sense 'is necessary/needed'. ز بهر سپاه is a purpose/benefit phrase 'for the army' (بهر = 'for', سپاه = 'army'). The whole yields 'from anything that is needed for the army.'
segments.s297-g51: فرستم is the first-person present (classical) 'I send' (often used for immediate/future action). همه 'all' is the object scope; همچنین is an adverb 'likewise/so' modifying the sending; پیش تو means 'in your presence/before you' (پیش = 'before/in front of', تو = 'you'). The phrase order is Subject-Verb-Object/Adverbial (verb precedes the locative).
segments.s297-g52: This is a coordinated noun phrase giving who is being sent: پسر پهلوان 'the son, a champion'. و links to پدر خویش تو which means 'your own father.' خوی + ش is the reflexive/possessive element (خوی = kinship/own; ش is the enclitic possessive), and the following تو specifies the person ('your'), a classical structure for 'your own father.'
segments.s297-g53: دو لشکر = 'two armies' (dual/collective noun + numeral). برآساید is a classical/invocational form of the verb 'آسودن' with the prefix بر-, here functioning optatively/impetratively 'may ... find relief' or 'be relieved.' از رنج رزم is an ablative phrase 'from the hardship of battle' (رنج رزم = 'toil/suffering of war').
segments.s297-g54: همه روز ما = 'all our days' (همه modifying روز, ما = our). بازگردد is the verb 'return' in a classical/optative sense 'may ... return.' به بزم = 'to the feast/banquet' (بزم = banquet/assembly). The clause expresses a wish that daily life revert to festive gatherings.
segments.s297-g55: ور is the archaic/poetic 'if.' ایدون (ایدَن) means 'it comes/happens' and here introduces the conditional clause ('if it happens that ...'). که is the subordinating 'that.' جان + ت + را is 'your life' with ت the 2nd-person suffix attached to جان and را the definite object marker; word order places the object before the agent name اهرمن (Ahriman) which will govern the verb in the next segment.
segments.s297-g56: بپیچد (from پیچیدن) is third-person present/subjunctive 'twists' or 'may twist'; همی is a classical adverbial particle (adds continuous/emphatic sense). تا introduces the result/purpose clause 'so that/so as to,' and بپوشی کفن is second-person subjunctive 'you put on/don the shroud' (کفن = shroud). Together: '... (Ahriman) twists (your life) so that you put on the shroud.'
segments.s297-g145: چو is a subordinating particle meaning 'when' at the start of a temporal clause. بشنید is the past tense (3rd singular) 'he heard'; classical Persian often places the verb before the subject (V S O order), so خسرو follows the verb as the subject. ز introduces the source/agent 'from' (poetic use) and links سخن to قارن: 'speech from Qaran' (i.e. Qaran's words).
segments.s297-g146: به یاد آمدش is an impersonal/experiential construction literally 'it came to his memory' = 'he remembered' or 'it occurred to him'; آمدش is آمد + enclitic pronoun -ش 'to him'. گفتهای کهن functions as a noun phrase 'old/ancient sayings' (گفتهای here = 'utterances/sayings') that is the semantic thing which 'came to mind'.
segments.s297-g147: بخندید is past tense 'he laughed' but with ز + noun it takes the sense 'laughed at' (laughed because of / at). Classical word order places the verb first (بخندید) then the subject (خسرو). ز introduces the cause/target of ridicule ('at / because of the deed of Niyâ').
segments.s297-g148: از آن جستن is a verbal-nominal phrase: از آن ('from that' / 'at that') + جستن (infinitive/gerund 'seeking, trying'); together 'that attempt to seek'. چاره و کیمیا are the objects of جستن joined by و ('a remedy and the elixir' — کیمیا used metaphorically as a miraculous cure). The whole phrase explains what he laughed at.
segments.s297-g149: از آن پس is an adverbial phrase 'after that' / 'thereafter'. چنین 'thus' modifies the following speech. گفت is the past-3rd-singular 'said' (classical V S order: verb then subject) and کافراسیاب is the speaker's name; the clause reports his words.
segments.s297-g150: پشیمان شدست is an archaic/perfective compound meaning 'he became remorseful / he repents' (شدست = شد + است contracted). از introduces the reason/cause ('because of' or 'for') and is followed by the verbal noun گذشتن 'crossing' (here a gerund meaning 'having crossed') and ز آب 'over the water' (ز + noun = 'across/from the water'). Together: 'he is repentant for crossing the water.'
segments.s297-g151: ورا is a dative/locative particle 'to him/there' marking the person concerned. چشم بی‌آب is a compound adjective-noun meaning 'an eye without water' (i.e. tearless/dry-eyed). و joins لب پر سخن 'a lip full of speech' — here پر follows the noun to mean 'full (of)'; سخن is 'words/speech'. The phrase is poetic: two attributes (dry eye, talkative lip) assigned to that person.
segments.s297-g152: مرا دل is the older order for 'my heart' (مرا = 'to me / my' functioning as possessor; را is the definite/object marker appearing in the segmentation). The nominal predicate is 'دل پر از ...' = 'heart full of ...' where پر takes a following از-phrase. دردهای کهن is the plural genitive 'ancient pains/sorrows' specifying the content of that fullness.
segments.s297-g105: به ره با طلایه بر آویختند is a clause with two prepositional/ adverbial phrases followed by a past-tense verb. به ره (preposition + noun) means 'along/on the road' or 'toward the route'; با طلایه is an accompaniment/instrumental phrase 'with the vanguard'; بر آویختند is the past 3rd-person-plural verb (بر + آویختند) meaning 'they hung/placed/posted' (here: deployed or set up). Word order is typical Persian: adverbial phrases before the verb; آویختند is archaic/poetic but functions as ordinary past 'they hung/attached/posted'.
segments.s297-g106: به نام از پی شیده خون ریختند: به نام here is an adverbial phrase 'for the sake of a name' i.e. 'for the sake of renown/fame'. از پی is a fixed prepositional sequence meaning 'in pursuit of / after', followed by شیده (here the proper name Shideh). خون is the object 'blood' and ریختند is the past 3rd-plural verb 'they poured/shed'. Overall: 'for the sake of fame, in pursuit of Shideh, they shed blood.'
segments.s297-g107: تنی چند از ایرانیان خسته شد: تنی چند is an idiomatic quantifier 'a few (persons)' with تنی functioning as 'some/bodies' and چند 'several'; از ایرانیان links the group ('of the Iranians'); خسته شد is the predicate 'became tired' (شد as past auxiliary 'became'). Note that تنی چند is treated grammatically as a unit so the verb is singular (شد).
segments.s297-g108: وز آن روی پیکار پیوسته شد: وز آن روی is a poetic/conjunctive phrase meaning 'and/for that reason' (equivalent to 'از آن روی'); پیکار 'battle/fighting' is the subject; پیوسته شد is 'became continuous/continued' (پیوسته functioning as adjective/adverb before شد). The clause means the fighting continued or became constant as a consequence.
segments.s297-g109: هم اندر زمان شیده آنجا رسید: هم 'also/at the same time' + اندر زمان 'in the time/moment' places the timing; شیده here is the proper name Shideh (arriving); آنجا 'there'; رسید is past 'arrived' (3rd singular). The clause reads 'at that same moment Shideh arrived there.'
segments.s297-g110: نگهبان ایرانیان را بدید: نگهبان is the subject 'the guard/sentinel'; ایرانیان را marks the definite object 'the Iranians' with the object marker را; بدید is an older/poetic past form meaning 'saw' (3rd singular). Word order is subject–object–verb as in Persian poetry.
segments.s297-g111: دل شیده گشت اندر آن کار تنگ: دل 'heart' is the subject; شیده here refers to Shideh (proper name/epithet) and forms a genitival/epithet 'Shideh's heart' or 'the love-struck heart'; گشت is past 'became'; اندر آن کار تنگ 'in that matter tight/narrow' (تنگ metaphorically 'distressed, constrained'). The clause means his heart was troubled by that affair.
segments.s297-g112: همی باز خواند آن یلان را ز جنگ: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('immediately/at once' in this context), باز 'back'/'again' marks return, خواند (past 3rd-sing) here means 'called' or 'summoned', آن یلان را is the definite object 'those heroes' with را, and ز جنگ is the prepositional phrase 'from the battle' (ز = از 'from' in poetic diction). Together: 'He at once called those heroes back from the fight.'
segments.s297-g153: بکوشد is an archaic/hortative form (ب- prefix + کوشد) used here as a jussive/optative 'let me try' / 'I will try'. که تا is a double purposive link ('that / so that') introducing purpose. دل is the direct object 'heart' (emotion, will). بپیچاندم is a first‑person verbal form with the b‑prefix + past stem + ‑م (= 'that I may turn/twist'); here it means 'may I bend/turn (his) heart' (figurative).
segments.s297-g154: به here is an instrumental/adverbial 'by/with'. بیشی means 'excess / exaggeration' (here: overstating or showing superiority). لشکر is 'army/host' (direct object). بترساندم is first person past/optative 'I will frighten' (b‑prefix + ترساندم) — together: 'by exaggeration I shall frighten the army.'
segments.s297-g155: بدان گه که functions as a temporal clause 'at that time when' — here بدَان (an archaic/poetic combo) + گه 'time/occasion' and که introducing the relative clause. گردنده is a present‑participle 'turning/revolving' qualifying چرخ (wheel); چرخ بلند 'the high/lofty wheel' is a poetic image for heaven or the wheel of fate.
segments.s297-g156: نگردد is a negative/optative or narrative form of گردیدن 'to turn' meaning 'does not/will not turn' (here: 'refuses to turn'). ببایست is 'must/ought' (impersonal necessity). روز گزند = 'a day of harm/calamity' (روز = day/time, گزند = harm). The clause therefore states the inevitable consequence when the wheel stops turning.
segments.s297-g157: کنون 'now' sets the time. چاره ما 'our remedy/means' is the subject. جز این نیست is a fixed negational phrase 'there is nothing except this' (جز = except; این = this; نیست = does not exist). روی here is a poetic nominal 'course/way' (literally 'face/side') meaning 'the course to take'.
segments.s297-g158: که introduces the content/purpose of that course: 'that'. من 'I' is subject. دل پر از کین is a noun phrase 'a heart full of kīn' (پر = full (of), از = 'of' marking the content, کین = hatred/vengeance). شوم is a subjunctive/optative 'may I become' or 'I should be' (first person). پیش اوی means 'before him / in his presence' (پیش = before/in front of; اوی = him — poetic form).
segments.s297-g159: بگردم here is a first‑person aorist/subjunctive with b‑prefix 'let me go / I will go/turn' indicating the speaker's intended action. به آورد (آورد) is 'to bring / to bring about' — in this clause the sense is causative/resultive: 'to bring (him) to [engage]'. با او به جنگ = 'to war with him / into battle with him' (با او = with him; به جنگ = to battle). The whole segment expresses the speaker's resolve to go and engage him in battle.
segments.s297-g160: به هنگام کوشش = 'at the time of effort/when striving' (به هنگام = at the time of; کوشش = attempt/effort). نسازم is negative of ساختن 'to make/do' with first‑person ending (نـسازم = 'I will not make/perform'); paired with درنگ ('delay, pause') it yields 'I will not delay / I will not hesitate'. The clause states he will not tarry when action is required.
segments.s297-g1: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' (prep + enclitic). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' and here follows بدو in the inverted poetic order ("to him said the king"). شاه‌ای ... مرد is a vocative phrase: شاه (the speaker/agent 'the king') plus the addressive 'ای' introducing سرفراز مرد ('O exalted/valiant man').
segments.s297-g2: نه ... نه ... is the correlative 'neither ... nor ...'. گرم آزموده is a compound adjective phrase: گرم ('ardent/fervent') modified by the past participle آزموده ('tried/experienced') with the source phrase ز گیتی ('by/from the world') specifying how he is "tried." سرد is the contrasting adjective 'cold/aloof.' The whole phrase characterizes the addressee's temperament.
segments.s297-g3: از ایدر ('from here') gives the starting point; ایدر is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'here.' برو is the second-person singular imperative 'go.' تا میان سپاه ('as far as / into the middle of the host') specifies the destination: تا + میان سپاه = 'to the middle of the army.' The verb imperative appears early, typical of terse commands.
segments.s297-g4: از ایشان ('from among them') marks the source for the request. یکی مرد دانا is the noun phrase 'one/ a certain wise man' (یکی = one/some, modified by مرد دانا = 'a wise man'). بخواه is the imperative 'ask/request' (2nd person), so the command is 'ask them for one wise man.'
segments.s297-g5: به کیخسرو is the dative phrase 'to Kay Khosrow.' از من ('from me') identifies the sender. پیامی is the direct object 'a message.' رسان is the imperative form 'deliver' (or 'see that ... is delivered'); together the clause is an imperative instruction to convey a message.
segments.s297-g6: که introduces the reported content 'that...'. گیتی is the subject 'the world.' جز این ('except this' / 'other than this') qualifies what follows. دارد is the present-tense verb 'has' (a general-stative present here). آیین و سان ('custom and guise/manner') is the compound object/predicate: the world 'has no custom or manner other than this.' Poetic word order places the verb before the nouns.
segments.s297-g7: نبیره ('scion/offspring') is the head noun; که introduces a relative clause describing that scion. رزم آورد is a verbal clause 'brought/ brings battle' (آورد = 'bring/cause'). با نیا is a prepositional phrase 'with (his) ancestor/ancestry' (با = with, نیا = ancestor); in poetry such phrases sometimes indicate origin or accompaniment. The clause as a whole modifies نبیره.
segments.s297-g8: دلش is the possessed noun 'his heart' (دل + enclitic -ش). بر here means 'toward' or 'set upon' and takes the noun بدی ('evil/wrongdoing') as its object. باشد is a subjunctive/present-type copula ('is/let it be'), stating the scion's disposition. و کیمیا adds a second object 'and alchemy' (کیمیا can mean literal alchemy or metaphorically the alchemical quest for transmutation/secret wealth).
segments.s297-g121: که is the relative/connector 'who/that' introducing a clause describing someone. پیغمبر شاه توران سپاه is a noun group in apposition: پیغمبر (literally 'prophet' but here 'messenger/agent') + شاه توران ('king of Turan') + سپاه ('host, army') — i.e. 'the messenger of the king of Turan's army.' Word order is noun + modifiers (possession follows the possessed phrase in Persian poetry).
segments.s297-g122: گوی is used as a noun/participle 'speaking/uttering' here (sense 'saying'), followed by بر + منش indicating direction/target ('to Manesh' or 'toward Manesh'); با درفشی سیاه is an instrumental phrase 'with a black standard' modifying the messenger's action. Note the compact, poetic word order: action/participle (گوی) then direction (بر منش) then accompaniment (با درفشی سیاه).
segments.s297-g123: همی is an adverb meaning 'continually/keeps (on)' and qualifies گوید ('says/keeps saying'). شیده is an epithet 'love‑struck' (here a person described as shideh). که introduces the content clause, and هستم is the first‑person present of 'to be' embedded in indirect speech ('that I am ...'); به نام is the prepositional phrase 'by the name (of)'. The clause is indirect reported speech after گوید.
segments.s297-g124: کسی 'someone' is the indefinite noun, بایدم is the poetical/colloquial form of باید + enclitic -م 'I must', تا introduces purpose 'so that / in order that', گزارم is the subjunctive/intentional form 'I may deliver / I may bring' (here 'to deliver'), and پیام is 'message.' Together the clause expresses necessity/purpose: 'I must [so that] I may deliver the message.'
segments.s297-g125: دل شاه (heart of the king) is the subject and شد is the past/resultative 'became.' زان سخن 'from that speech' gives the cause, and پر ز شرم is a predicative phrase 'full of shame' (پر 'full (of)' preceded by the poetic 'ز' meaning 'from/because of'). The order is subject – copula – cause – predicative complement, typical of Persian poetic compression.
segments.s297-g126: فرو ریخت is a compound verb 'fell/poured down' (فرو 'down' + ریخت 'fell/poured') describing tears; از دیدگان 'from the eyes' marks source, and آب گرم 'warm water' is the poetic expression for tears. The verb ریخت is used in the sense of 'to pour/flow' here, and the whole phrase is a simple past narrative clause following the previous line.
segments.s297-g127: چنین گفت is a formula 'thus said' introducing direct speech. کین here is a demonstrative/poetic pointer 'this' applied to شیده (Shideh/the love‑struck one), and خال منست is a nominal clause meaning 'the mole is mine' (منست is a poetic/archaic copular/adjectival form asserting possession/association). Word order follows the poetical preference for short, emphatic phrases.
segments.s297-g128: به بالا is an adjectival/locative phrase 'of high/upper place' (here 'high‑born' or 'lofty'), و 'and' connects the next noun, مردی 'a man', and همال منست is a verblike phrase meaning 'is equal/peer (of me)' — همال here carrying the sense 'one of equal standing/peer.' The clause continues the character/praise of the person referenced in the preceding quoted speech.
segments.s297-g217: This is a conditional protasis + verbal clause. گر introduces a conditional "if." از من is a simple agent/source phrase "against/from me." همی before جست is the archaic/progressive particle meaning "still/keeps on," so همی جست = "keeps trying/leaping (at)." خواهد is a future/modal auxiliary (“he will”), and نبرد here is the verb-form used with خواهد to indicate the action (in classical style); together خواهد + نبرد yields a future assertion ("he will (not) take/fight/carry off") depending on context.
segments.s297-g218: شما را is accusative/emphatic address to the addressee(s) (“you”), with را as the object/topic marker. چرا is the interrogative "why." شد is the past/become verb (here "has become"), followed by چنین "thus/so" and روی زرد "a yellow/pale face" (روی = face; زرد = pale/wan). The clause asks why such paleness has appeared on you.
segments.s297-g219: همی here functions as an emphatic/attentive particle (indeed/just). از شما means "from you/about you." این شگفت is the noun phrase "this wonder/marvel." آیدم is an archaic/clitic construction: آید ("it comes") + -م marking "to me," so the literal structure is "this wonder comes to me."
segments.s297-g220: همان is a demonstrative/adverb meaning "the same/that very." کین پیشین = "the former/old hatred/vengeance." بیفزایدم is first-person singular present/future of افزودن (بیفزاید with archaic prefix), meaning "I will add/increase" or "I shall add (to it)." Grammatically this is a main-clause verb stating the speaker's intent.
segments.s297-g221: گمانی here is a noun meaning "a supposition/guess" and begins a negated idiom. نبردم is a classical/periphrastic past first-person form tied to گمانی, here functioning idiomatically as "I did not suppose/think" (lit. "I took no such thought"). که introduces the subordinate clause "that ..." and ایرانیان is the subject of that subordinate clause.
segments.s297-g222: گشایند is a present/subjunctive 3rd-person plural of گشودن (to open/loosen), used here for the Iranians' action "would open/loosen." جاوید is an adjective "eternal/for ever." ز is the poetic preposition "from/because of," and این کین میان = "this enmity between (them)" (میان = between). The clause thus states what the speaker did not suppose the Iranians would do regarding this lasting enmity.
segments.s297-g223: کسی را ندیدم is a negated existential/object clause: کسی را (object-marked "anyone/no one") + ندیدم ("I did not see"). ز ایران سپاه is a genitive/ablative phrase "from the Iranian army/host." The whole segment thus means "I saw no one of the Iranian host."
segments.s297-g224: که is the relative pronoun "who/that." افگنده بود is a compound past: افگنده (a participle meaning "placed/laid/deployed/strewn") + بود (past auxiliary "was"), so "who had been placed/deployed." اندر این رزمگاه = "in this battlefield/field of combat." The relative clause modifies کسی/سپاه.
segments.s297-g89: سران را (object marked by را) is the definite/explicit object 'the chiefs' or 'the leaders'. به یاری is a prepositional phrase 'with help' where به functions adverbially ('by/with'). برون is an adverb 'out/forth' placed before the verb. آوریم is 1st person plural present/subjunctive/optative (classical Persian) meaning 'we bring' / 'let us bring' / 'we will bring' and completes the SOV-style clause (prepositional phrase + object + verb).
segments.s297-g90: به جوی اندرون is a locative prepositional phrase 'into/ to the inner stream' — به here denotes direction/into. اندرون follows the noun jوی as a postpositive adjective/qualifier 'inner'. آب و خون is a coordinated direct object 'water and blood'. آوریم again is the same 1pl verb form at the end of the clause, giving the expected Persian order (PP/objects ... verb).
segments.s297-g91: چو here functions as the conditional conjunction 'if/when'. بدخواه is the subject 'the ill-wisher' and پیغام تو is the object 'your message' (possessive تو attached to پیغام). نشنود is 3rd person singular negative present (classical form) of شنیدن 'does not hear' — the clause is prototypical 'If X does not hear Y'.
segments.s297-g92: بپیچد is a 3rd person singular present/subjunctive or jussive of پیچیدن 'turn/withdraw' meaning 'let him turn (away)'. The following sequence (ب + دین) in the tokenization corresponds in the text to بدین/بدین meaning 'thus/with this' (here treated as 'by/with these words'); گفتها is the plural 'utterances/these words'. نگرود is a subjunctive/optative form meaning 'do not accept/embrace' (from the root given); together the clause issues the instruction 'let him turn away; do not accept these sayings.'
segments.s297-g93: به تنها تن خویش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by/with your single body' or 'with only your person' — به used adverbially ('by/with'), تنها modifies تن ('alone, single'), and خویش (here split into خوی + ش) yields the reflexive/possessive 'your own'. از او رزم خواه is an instruction 'seek battle from/with him' where خواه is an auxiliary/optative 'desire/seek' used as a hortative (imperative-like) verb.
segments.s297-g94: به دیدار دوراز میان سپاه is a locative/goal phrase 'to/for a meeting far from the midst of the army'. به marks the direction/goal ('to' or 'at'), دیدار is 'meeting', دوراز ('دور از') is a compound adjective 'far from', and میان سپاه means 'among/in the midst of the host' — together 'to a meeting far from the army.' Word order places the goal phrase before the verb in the surrounding line.
segments.s297-g95: پسر is the subject 'the son'. آفرین کرد is a light-verb construction: آفرین here is an interjection 'bravo' (or exclamation) and کرد is the auxiliary 'did/said', so 'the son exclaimed “bravo”'. و آمد برون is a simple coordinated clause 'and came out' with آمد (past) and adverb برون 'out'.
segments.s297-g96: پدر is the subject 'the father'. دیده پر آب literally 'eye(s) full of water' (idiomatically 'eyes full of tears'); here دیده is noun and پر is the stative adjective 'full (of)'. و links to دل پر ز خون 'and heart full of blood' where ز (classical form of از) marks source/reason 'with/of blood'. This is a compressed poetic description: two nominal phrases with پر + ز marking fullness.
segments.s297-g73: This is two short coordinated clauses compressed into one line. پدر پیر شد is a straightforward past clause: subject (پدر) + adjective predicate (پیر) + past auxiliary/verb شد 'became/got'. پایمردش جوان is a juxtaposed nominal clause: پایمردش is پایمرد (a stalwart/loyal one) with the third‑person possessive suffix -ش 'his', and جوان follows as an adjectival or nominal predicate; the copula is omitted (a common poetic device), so it reads 'his stalwart was young.'
segments.s297-g74: جوانی is a noun 'youth' or 'a youth' giving a more explicit identification of the preceding 'جوان'. خردمند and روشن‌روان are attributive adjectives placed after the noun (typical in Persian poetic phrasing): خردمند 'wise', و links the two adjectives, روشن‌روان is a compound epithet 'bright‑souled' or 'clear‑minded.' The whole phrase apposes and further describes the young man.
segments.s297-g75: به آوردگه is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the arena or field' (به + آوردگه). با تو جنگ آورد is a compact verb phrase meaning 'made war with you' or 'came to fight you': با تو marks the opponent, جنگ is 'battle/fight' used as the object/event, and آورد is the past verb 'brought/caused' used here idiomatically as 'waged' or 'engaged in'.
segments.s297-g76: دلیرست is an adjective 'is brave' with the copula compressed into -ست (poetic form of است). و links the clauses. جنگ پلنگ آورد is elliptic: 'in battle (he) brought a panther' — پلنگ can be literal or a metaphorical epithet for a fierce warrior; آورد again is the past verb 'brought' (here 'brought/produced' or 'displayed').
segments.s297-g77: ببینیم is first‑person plural jussive/optative 'let us see' or 'we shall see.' تا introduces the subordinate clause here meaning 'so that / in order that' or simply 'to/that' in the sense 'let us see who...'. The subordinate clause has inverted/poetic order: بر (on/upon) + که (who) + گردد (subjunctive/present 'will turn/shall become') + سپهر (the subject 'the sky/heavens') — literally 'on whom the sky will turn.'
segments.s297-g78: This clause continues the question of 'whom': که را ('whom' with the definite/object marker را for emphasis), بر نهد is a verb phrase 'will place upon' (نهد = 'to set/place'), بر سر 'upon the head' is the locative phrase, and از تاج مهر 'of/from the crown of favor' specifies which crown. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect, but the sense is 'on whose head the crown of favor will be set.'
segments.s297-g79: ور is a poetic conditional 'if.' ایدون is a temporal verb form meaning 'when he comes/arrives' (archaic/poetic). که introduces the subordinate clause. با او is the prepositional phrase 'with him,' نجویی here is a classical/poetic form meaning 'you seek/pursue (battle)' but begins with ن- making it negative in this construction (read as 'you do not seek'); نبرد is the noun 'battle' used as the object. Together: 'if, when he comes, you do not seek battle with him.'
segments.s297-g80: دگرگونه 'differently/otherwise' is an adverbial modifier. خواهی here is the auxiliary/future sense 'you will' (from خواستن used periphrastically), همی is an emphatic/progressive particle common in classical Persian, and کار کرد literally 'do the work/deed' (کار = affair/deed, کرد = to do). Together they form the periphrastic future: 'you will do things differently' / 'you will act otherwise.'
segments.s297-g129: نگه کرد is a past-tense verbal phrase 'he looked/cast a glance' (کرد as the past auxiliary). گردنکشی is a noun used adverbially here meaning 'defiance' or 'a defiant bearing' and is the thing shown by the glance. زان میان = ز + آن + میان (poetic zān) means 'from among/from that middle', giving the source/place of the look.
segments.s297-g130: نبد is an archaic/poetic negative past (= نبود) meaning 'there was not' or 'there was none'. پیش here means 'in front of / before (him) / in the presence', and جز = 'except'. قارن کاویان is a proper epithet ('Qārān of the Kayanian [dynasty]') serving as the sole exception.
segments.s297-g131: بدو here is the clitic phrase 'to him / unto him' (indirect object). گفت is the past 'said' (the provided sense list did not include the verbal sense, so the exact sense index is not applicable). رو is an imperative 'go/face' (classical literary imperative, not the nominal senses given), پیش او = 'before him / into his presence', and شادکام is used vocatively (a name or epithet meaning 'fortunate').
segments.s297-g132: درودش ده is an imperative clause: درود (greeting) with the object suffix -ش = 'to him', and ده = 'give' (imperative). از ما 'from us' marks the source of the greeting. و = 'and' links the next imperative; بشنو is an imperative 'listen!' and پیام is its object 'the message' (i.e. 'hear the message').
segments.s297-g133: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. قارن is the proper name Qārān, and بیامد is the past 'came'. ز پیش سپاه = 'from before the host/army' (ز + پیش = out of/in front of the army).
segments.s297-g134: بدید is the past 'he saw'. آن is the demonstrative 'that/the'. درفشان here is an adjectival/epithet meaning 'shining, glittering' modifying درفش 'banner/standard', and سیاه 'black' is the postpositive adjective specifying its color.
segments.s297-g135: چو آمد = 'when (he) came'. بر + شیده = 'to/unto Shīdeh' (بر as a preposition 'to/upon' plus the name/epithet شیده). دادش is the past 'gave' with the object suffix -ش 'him', and درود is the direct object 'greeting'.
segments.s297-g136: ز شاه و ز ایرانیان 'from the king and from the Iranians' names the source or sponsors of the salutations. برفزود is a classical past verb meaning 'he caused to be raised/added' and here functions to mean 'he bestowed/conveyed' these salutations on Shīdeh.
segments.s297-g81: بمان is an imperative ('stay, remain') addressed to someone. تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning 'until'. بیاساید here is a 3rd-person present/optative form meaning 'may rest / rests' and completes the 'تا' clause. امشب (time adverb) precedes the subject سپاه (army), so the literal order is 'until rests tonight the army' — English reorders this to 'until the army rests tonight.'
segments.s297-g82: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. نهد is 3rd-person singular ('he places/sets'); the prepositional phrase بر سر ('upon the head') appears before the verb, and the object کلاه ('helmet') comes at the end, modified by the preceding phrase کوه زرین ('mountain(‑like) golden') as a poetic compound. The whole clause reads 'when he sets the golden (mountain‑like) helmet upon his head.'
segments.s297-g83: ز (poetic 'from') introduces the source 'لشکر' (the army). گزینیم is first‑person plural ('we choose' / 'we will choose') placed before the object — classical Persian often allows verb before its object — and جنگاوران is the object 'warriors.' The line literally reads 'from the army we choose warriors.'
segments.s297-g84: سرافراز is an epithet ('proud/triumphant') that modifies the previously mentioned جنگاوران; با introduces an instrumental/associative phrase 'with'. گرزهای is the plural 'maces' (with the plural marker -های) and گران is a postpositive adjective 'heavy' (or 'valuable'), so با گرزهای گران means 'armed with heavy maces.'
segments.s297-g85: زمین را uses the object marker را to mark 'the earth' as the thing affected. ز خون places the material/source 'from blood' before رود (here a noun 'river') and دریا کنیم is the verbal nucleus 'we will make (into) a sea.' The sequence is poetic piling of images: 'we will make the earth a river/sea of blood,' with کنیم (first‑person plural) as the main verb.
segments.s297-g86: ز بالای is a prepositional phrase 'from the top/height of ...' with بالای ('above/height') governing the following noun بدخواه ('ill‑wisher, enemy') in a possessive/genitive relation (classical Persian often omits ezâfe spelling). پهنا کنیم ('we will make/extend breadth') is the verb phrase meaning 'we will widen/extend (our) expanse.' Together: 'from the heights of the ill‑wishers we will extend our breadth.'
segments.s297-g87: دوم روز is an ordinal + noun ('second day') with the adjective before the noun in classical style. هنگام is a temporal preposition 'at the time (of)', and بانگ خروس is a genitive construction 'the cry of the rooster' (بانگ preceding خروس). The whole phrase sets the time: 'on the second day at the rooster's cry.'
segments.s297-g88: ببندیم is first‑person plural jussive/optative ('we will bind/fasten' or 'let us fasten'). بر introduces the site 'on/upon', کوهه (a small hill/brow) together with پیل ('of the elephant') forms the locative 'on the elephant's brow' (classical genitive without written ezâfe), and کوس is the direct object 'war‑drum' that is being fastened. Word order places the verb first and the locative + object after, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s297-g137: جوان (subject) + نیز (adverb 'also') precede the past verbal form بگشاد (from گشودن) here meaning 'opened' idiomatically 'opened his mouth / began to speak'. شیرین‌زبان is a compound epithet (شیرین 'sweet' modifying زبان 'speech/tongue') meaning 'sweet‑tongued' or 'eloquent.' Word order (S adv V NP) is typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s297-g138: که introduces a descriptive clause ('who / for he'), followed by the adjectival phrase بیدار دل (literally 'awake‑hearted' = alert in feeling). بود is the past copula 'was'. و روشن‌روان links a second predicate adjective (روشن‌روان, a poetic compound meaning 'bright‑souled' or 'clear‑minded'). The whole clause gives two coordinated qualities of the subject.
segments.s297-g139: بگفت is a past tense reporting verb 'he said / he told.' آنچه introduces a free relative 'what / that which' and بشنید is a past verb 'he heard' (so 'what he had heard'). ز + افراسیاب marks the source 'from Afrasiab.' The clause is an object of بگفت.
segments.s297-g140: The initial ز here functions as 'concerning / about' (governing a list). The nouns آرام, بزم, رزم, شتاب enumerate topics ('peace/calm', 'feasting/assembly', 'battle', 'haste') joined by و. The token وز appears as a poetic orthographic variant/connector; in this edition it separates items but does not carry the literal senses listed for the lemma.
segments.s297-g141: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' placed before the verb بشنید ('he heard'), a common classical order. The name قارن appears after the verb (classical narratives often place the verb early). سخنهای نغز is the object phrase 'pithy/delicate words' with نغز qualifying سخنها.
segments.s297-g142: از marks the source 'from/of' and آن نامور 'that famed one' names the source. بخرد پاک مغز are a string of epithets: بخرد 'wise', پاک 'pure', مغز 'brained/core' (i.e. 'pure‑minded' or 'of sound mind'). The adjective sequence is appositive/descriptive of the named person.
segments.s297-g143: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'he came' (arrived). بر شاه ایران is a prepositional phrase 'to/ before the King of Iran' (بر here functions as 'to/onto/toward' in classical usage). The following بگفت 'he said' narrates the speech after the arrival; two verbs in sequence describe successive actions.
segments.s297-g144: که introduces reported content 'that'. پیغامها is the subject 'the messages.' با خرد literally 'with wisdom' functions adverbially 'in accordance with wisdom' or 'reasonable.' بود is the past copula 'were.' جفت ('pair') is used adjectivally/idiomatically to mean 'matched/consistent' — 'were matched with wisdom.'
segments.s297-g233: ستوده is a past-participle adjective 'praised' placed before the noun as an epithet; جهاندار is the noun 'sovereign' (literally 'holder of the world'); برتر منش is a post‑nominal compound (برتر 'superior' + منش 'character') that qualifies جهاندار, so the whole phrase = 'the praised sovereign of superior character.'
segments.s297-g234: نخواهد is the third‑person negative modal ('he will not/would not wish'); که introduces a subordinate clause; بر ما بود is a copular construction with بر + pronoun + بود meaning 'be upon us' (بود here functions as the subjunctive/optative copula in literary Persian); سرزنش is the noun 'reproach' as the complement of بود.
segments.s297-g235: گویند is the impersonal or historical‑present 'they say' (classical narrator's formula); از ایران سواری نبود is a negative past statement 'there was no horseman from/among the Iranians' (نبود = past negative of بودن).
segments.s297-g236: که introduces a relative clause 'who'; یارست functions as a predicative phrase 'was a yār' (here 'یار' = match/companion) where the copula is contracted into the form; با شیده is the instrumental/associative 'with Shideh' (شیده here refers to the person attacked by the epithet 'shideh'); رزم آزمود is a verbal phrase 'tried/experienced battle' (آزمود past tense: 'he fought/tested himself in battle').
segments.s297-g237: که again introduces a clause; آمد is simple past 'came'; سواری is the subject 'a horseman/rider' (poetic word order places the subject after the verb); به دشت نبرد is a prepositional phrase 'into the plain/field of battle' (به ... دشت = 'into the plain').
segments.s297-g238: جز از شاهشان is an exception phrase 'except their king' (جز = 'except', followed by از with the possessive form شاهشان); این دلیری نکرد is a past negative clause 'did not do/show this bravery' — literal order 'this bravery did not do' where نکرد is the past negative of کردن and the whole means 'none showed such courage.'
segments.s297-g239: نخواهد is the negative modal 'would not/will not desire'; مگر here means 'except'; خسرو موبدان is the exception phrase — خسرو can be read as the name/title and موبدان 'the mobeds/priestly class' is attached, so together they indicate the sole party who would desire the outcome.
segments.s297-g240: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'; بر ما بود is again 'be upon us' (بود as literary copula); ننگ 'shame' is the predicate; تا جاودان is an adverbial phrase 'until/for eternity' (= 'forever'), so the clause means 'that shame be upon us forever.'
segments.s297-g169: همی + خواهد is a poetic/periphrastic present indicating repeated or habitual will: 'keeps wanting / intends continually.' از here is a prepositional marker introducing the opponent ('against / with respect to the King of Iran'). نبرد is a noun 'battle/fight' and is the object of خواهد.
segments.s297-g170: بدان is here an adverbial/demonstrative phrase ('to that / for that end') formed from ب + دان and links to the purpose clause introduced by تا. تا + کند introduces purpose/result 'so that he may make...'; کند is the subjunctive/optative 3rd‑person form of کردن 'to make/do'. روز ما را is a definite object with را; به درد is a prepositional phrase 'into/into pain' expressing the resulting state.
segments.s297-g171: تو is the 2nd‑person subject. بر تیزی او places action 'against his sharpness' (بر = 'against / in face of'); دلیری here denotes bold action/valor and is negated by the prohibitive مکن ('do not'). The phrase is an imperative prohibition: 'do not be bold against his sharpness.'
segments.s297-g172: از ایران is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of Iran' (here idiomatically part of the warnings). وز appears as an archaic or bound form of و + ز (i.e. 'and from') linking two prohibitions. تاج سیری مکن is another prohibition: تاج سیری = 'satisfaction/regard with the crown' (سیری = satiety/satisfaction) and مکن gives 'do not be satisfied/comfortable with the crown.'
segments.s297-g173: وگر = 'if / but if'. شیده here is a personal name (Shideh) acting as the subject; از شاه جوید نبرد means 'seeks/requests battle from the king' (جوید is present/factual 'seeks'). The clause sets a conditional: 'if Shideh seeks battle of the king...'.
segments.s297-g174: به آورد here means 'brings about / causes' (آورد in sense 'to cause/bring about'); گستاخ qualifies the manner as 'impudent/audacious.' با او مگرد is an admonition directed at the addressee: با او + (مگرد) = 'do not be/act with him (impudently)' — مگرد is an archaic/formal prohibitive/negative imperative form.
segments.s297-g175: به دست تو‌گر is a conditional circumstantial phrase: به دست تو + گر (تو گر = 'if you') meaning 'if by your hand / if it be by you.' گردد is the literary present/subjunctive 'becomes / happens' and تباه is the predicate adjective 'ruined / ruined utterly.' The whole means 'if Shideh be ruined by your hand.'
segments.s297-g176: یکی نامور = 'one famed/renowned (person)' (یکی = 'one / a certain'); کم شود is a common expression 'be diminished / be lost' (کم = 'less / lacking', شود = 'becomes'), and زان سپاه = 'from that army/host' (زان = 'from that'). The clause therefore means 'one renowned man will be taken from that host.'
segments.s297-g25: همه ریگ و دریا is the subject phrase ('all the sand and the sea'). مرا is the dative/benefactive pronoun 'for me' (here split in the text as م + را), and لشکرند is a nominal predicate: لشکر (army/multitude) with the archaic/poetic plural/copular ending -ند = 'are an army'. The line thus says those things function as (an) army for the speaker.
segments.s297-g26: همه repeats and resumes the idea 'all (of them)'. نره is an adjective/qualifier 'male' (as in 'male lions') modifying شیر (here شیرند = 'are lions' or 'are valiant like lions'); the verb/ending -ند again marks the 3rd-person plural copular/nominal predicate. کنداورند is an archaic/compound verb meaning 'bring about / perform' or 'do (deeds)' and is coordinated with the previous predicate, implying they execute deeds or obey commands.
segments.s297-g27: هر آنگه که is a temporal/subordinating phrase meaning 'whenever' or 'whenever it is that'. فرمان دهم is the speaker's verb phrase 'I give the command' (دهم = 'I give'); کوه گنگ is a noun phrase 'the mute/silent mountain' (noun followed by adjective). The whole segment sets up a conditional/time-clause: whenever I order, (even) the mute mountain ...
segments.s297-g28: چو here functions as 'when/as' (introducing what happens on that occasion). دریا کند/کنند (دریا + کنند) is an elliptic/poetic construction meaning 'make (it) like the sea' or 'act as the sea does' with کنند = 3rd pl. of کردن used in a causative/result sense. ای پسر روز جنگ is a vocative address — 'O son (youth), (of) the day of battle' — with ای marking direct address.
segments.s297-g29: ولیکن = 'but/yet' introduces the contrast. همی is an archaic continuous/aspectual particle that precedes a present-tense verb to mean 'still/continually'; ترسم is 1st-person present 'I fear' (ترس‑ + ‑م). از کردگار = 'of/from the Creator' expresses the object/cause of the fear.
segments.s297-g30: ز خون ریختن is a prepositional + verbal-noun sequence: ز (poetic 'از') = 'from/because of', خون ریختن = the act of 'shedding blood' (ریختن is the infinitive/gerund 'to spill/shed'). وز بد روزگار is a poetic fusion (و + ز) meaning 'and from/because of' bad fate; بد روزگار = 'evil/bad fortune' or 'hard times'. Note the text uses poetic contractions and nominalized verbs as objects.
segments.s297-g31: که introduces the consequence/explanation 'that...'. چندین سر is a quantified noun phrase 'several/many heads'; نامور and بی‌گناه are adjectives following the noun (a common Persian order: noun + modifiers) meaning 'renowned' and 'innocent' respectively. Together they describe the victims whose fate is feared.
segments.s297-g32: جدا گردد is a passive/impersonal construction: جدا (apart/separated) + گردد (subjunctive/3rd-person of گشت/گردیدن = 'become/ happen') = 'may be separated' or 'be severed'. از تن = 'from the body'. بدین رزمگاه = 'on/at this battlefield' (بدین = به + این 'to/at this' in archaic/poetic form). Note the text's word-splitting in the provided list (ب + دین) — grammatically it is the single prepositional phrase 'بدین رزمگاه'.
segments.s297-g209: به کاووس = preposition + proper name: 'به' marks direction/toward and is placed before the name کاووس. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/altogether' modifying the following verb phrase. چه پوزش بریم functions as an interrogative clause: چه ('how') questions the manner of پوزش بریم, where پوزش 'an excuse/pardon' is the noun object and بریم is an optative/subjunctive first‑person‑plural form (classical usage of بردن/بـ‑ + past stem) meaning 'we should present/offer' (i.e. 'we make/offer an excuse').
segments.s297-g210: بدین دیدگان is a fused phrase: 'ب' here is the prepositional/prefix element (here the prefix 'به‑') and the whole phrase means 'to/with these eyes' (بدین = به + این). دیدگان is the plural/formal noun 'eyes' (instrumental). چون introduces a subordinate temporal/conditional sense 'when/as' and links to the verb بنگریم ('we look'); بدو = به + او 'to him' (direction toward him). بنگریم is present/subjunctive 1pl of نگرستن 'to look, to gaze' meaning 'we look/gaze (at him)'.
segments.s297-g211: شنیدی is the simple past (2nd singular) 'have you heard' (used rhetorically). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/what follows'. بر ایرج نیکبخت uses بر + proper name to mean 'upon/against Irāj' or 'befell Irāj', with نیکبخت an epithet 'fortunate' modifying ایرج.
segments.s297-g212: چه آمد = literal 'what came' but here idiomatically 'what happened/what befell'. به تور = 'at the hands of Tūr' (به marking the indirect object/agent in this narrative phrasing). از پی = 'in pursuit of/for the sake of' (از + پی), followed by تاج و تخت 'crown and throne' as the objects of pursuit. The verb آمد carries the meaning 'befell/happened to' in this context.
segments.s297-g213: سیاووش را shows the proper name marked with the direct‑object marker را. نیز 'also/likewise' adds that this is another such case. بر بیگناه uses بر + adjective بیگناه ('innocent') to indicate that he was killed despite being innocent (classical usage where بر can mark the circumstance or contrary condition).
segments.s297-g214: بکشت is an archaic past form 'he/they killed' (ب‑ prefix plus past stem کشت). از پی = 'because of / in pursuit of' (expressing motive), followed by a list of motives: گنج 'treasure', تخت 'throne', and کلاه 'cap/hat' (here 'headgear' or crown/helm). The coordination (و) simply links the nouns as reasons for the killing.
segments.s297-g215: فریبنده ترکی is an adjectival phrase: فریبنده 'deceptive' modifies ترکی 'a Turk' (here an agent/character). از آن انجمن 'from that assembly' uses از to mark origin and آن انجمن identifies the specific group/place he left.
segments.s297-g216: بیامد is the past tense 'came' (3rd person). خرامان is an adverb meaning 'with a proud/stately gait' and modifies the verb. به نزدیک من literally 'to near me' = 'approached me' or 'came close to me', where به marks direction and من is the object 'me'.
segments.s297-g185: همی is a habitual/continuative adverb meaning 'continually' or 'keeps on'. پوزش آرد is a 3rd-person singular verb phrase: آرد (brings/gives) governs پوزش (an excuse/apology), so literally 'he brings an excuse'. بدین بد is a prepositional phrase 'to this bad one' (ب = to/for + the following element), and که کرد introduces a reported clause 'that he did' (که = 'that', کرد = 'he did/made'). The whole segment = 'he keeps giving an excuse to this bad one, that he did...'.
segments.s297-g186: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' / 'out of' introducing a cause; بیچارگی is the noun 'helplessness/misery'. جست is the stem of جستن here in the sense of 'sought/attempted' (used with the auxiliary خواهد), خواهد is a future/modal auxiliary 'will' and نبرد is 'battle/ fight' (used as the event he will not take up). The clause reads 'out of helplessness he (claims) he sought/acted and will not enter battle.'
segments.s297-g187: همی again marks continuous action 'continually/keeps on'. گوید here functions as the verb 'says' (note: the listed senses were nominal, but in context گوید is 3rd-person present 'he/it says'). اسبان و گنج درم is a coordinated noun phrase 'horses and (the) treasure/hoard of silver' (درم = dirhams/coin).
segments.s297-g188: که introduces reported content 'that ...'. بنهاد is a perfect/stative form of نهادن 'put/placed' (here 'placed' or 'set'). تور is the proper name Tūr. از پی is a two-word locative/temporal phrase 'after / following' (از = from/out of; پی = after/following), and زادشم is a proper name (Zādsham) as the reference point. The phrase = 'that Tur placed (them) after Zādsham.'
segments.s297-g189: همان is a demonstrative/adjective 'that/the same'. تخت شاهی is a genitive/compound 'throne of kingship' = 'royal throne' (تخت = throne, شاهی = royal/kingship). و connects the next nominal: تاج سران = 'the crowns of the chiefs/heads' (تاج = crown, سران = leaders/chiefs). The word order is noun + attributive (تخت + شاهی) typical in classical Persian.
segments.s297-g190: کمرهای زرین is a noun phrase 'golden belts' (کمرها = belts/girdles, here plural; زرین = golden). و links to گرز گران = 'heavy/precious maces' (گرز = mace/club, گران = heavy/valuable). The adjectives follow the nouns (کمرهای زرین = 'belts that are golden').
segments.s297-g191: سپارد is a 3rd-person singular verb 'he entrusts / delivers'. به گنج تو is the indirect object 'to your treasure/treasury' (به = to, گنج = treasury/treasure, تو = you). از گنج خویش is the source phrase 'from his own treasure' (از = from, گنج = treasure, خوی + ش = 'own/his' where خوی = kin/possession root and ش = his). Word order: verb + goal + source (typical flexible Persian ordering).
segments.s297-g192: همی باز is an adverbial cluster 'again / once more' (همی = continually/again flavor, باز = again). خرد here is used in the sense 'small / diminished' (an adjectival/participial sense) meaning 'reduced' or 'made small'. ب + دین = به دین 'to the obligation/debt' (ب = to/for, دین = obligation/debt), رنج خویش = 'his own suffering/hardship' (رنج = suffering/hardship; خوی + ش = 'his own'). Together: 'again (he) reduces his obligation by this his suffering.'
segments.s297-g41: Nominal clause with the copula 'بود' after the noun phrase: 'برادر بود جهن و جنگی پشنگ' = 'There was a brother, Jahan, and a fighter, Pashang.' 'جنگی' is an attributive noun ('fighter/warrior') modifying the following proper name; the coordination '...جهن و جنگی پشنگ' lists two persons/roles.
segments.s297-g42: Relative clause introduced by 'که' modifying the preceding person (Pashang). 'در جنگ' is an adverbial phrase 'in battle'; the verb 'کند' (make/do) takes 'دریا' (the sea) as its object and 'کوه سنگ' as the result/complement: literally 'who in battle makes the sea (into) a mountain of stone.' The word order is typical poetic inversion (object before verb and then the resultant noun phrase).
segments.s297-g43: 'هر آن بوم و بر' = 'every such land and district'; 'کآن' is a fused relative element (essentially 'که آن' = 'which/that'); 'ز ایران' = 'from Iran' (poetic 'ز' = از). The final word 'نهی' here is an archaic/poetic verbal form in a relative clause meaning roughly 'is taken/removed' or 'is separated' — its exact lexical sense is archaic and not matched by the given modern glosses.
segments.s297-g44: 'به فرمان کنم' = 'I will (by command) cause/do': 'کنم' is 1st-person present/subjunctive used here with future force. 'آن ز ترکان تهی' = 'make that empty of Turks' — 'تهی' used predicatively with 'ز' meaning 'devoid of'; word order places the demonstrative 'آن' before the 'ز ترکان' complement.
segments.s297-g45: 'ز گنج نیاکان ما هرچه هست' = 'from the treasure of our ancestors, whatever there is.' 'ز' marks source/possession ('from/of'), 'هرچه هست' is a free relative ('whatever exists'). The phrase orders the source first then the quantifier for poetic emphasis.
segments.s297-g46: A coordinated list introduced by 'ز' ('from'): 'ز دینار وز تاج و تخت و نشست' names types of treasure. The middle token 'وز' is an archaic/fused form (essentially 'و ز' = 'and from'); the nouns that follow are the listed items (dinar, crown, throne, seat/assembly). The repeated particle 'ز' emphasizes each category as coming from the ancestral treasure.
segments.s297-g47: 'ز اسب و سلیح و ز بیش و ز کم' is another enumerative series introduced by 'ز' ('from'): 'from horses and weapons, and from more and from less' — 'سلیح' is an archaic form of 'سلاح' (weapons), and 'بیش و کم' is an idiom meaning 'more and less' (i.e., whatever surplus or shortfall). The repeated 'ز' marks each category as part of the inherited goods.
segments.s297-g48: Relative clause 'که میراث ماند از نیا زادشم' = 'which remains as inheritance from the ancestor Zadashm.' 'میراث ماند' is a light verb construction 'remain as inheritance'; 'از نیا' indicates source ('from the ancestor') and 'زادشم' is a proper name specified as the ancestor.
segments.s297-g33: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. از پیش من = از + پیش + من means 'from before me / in my presence'; Persian places these particles before the noun. بر نگردی is the negative present/subjunctive of گردیدن with the separable prefix بر- indicating 'turn back / retreat' (ن- negates it), and ز جنگ (poetic ز = از) marks 'from the battle'; the verb (نگردی) comes at the end as is normal.
segments.s297-g34: The clause begins with the same verb form نگردی (negative 'do not turn/retreat') as consequence language; همانا is an emphatic adverb 'verily/truly'. که introduces the result clause. آیدت is آید + enclitic -ت 'comes to you / befalls you' (verb before object clitic), and ننگ is the noun 'shame/disgrace' as the thing that will befall.
segments.s297-g35: چو here = 'when' (sometimes 'if'); با من = 'with me'. به سوگند uses به as the adverbial/instrumental 'by/with' (به + noun) to mean 'by oath'. پیمان کنی is a present/subjunctive verb phrase 'you make a covenant' with the verb کنی (کردن) placed at the end.
segments.s297-g36: بکوشی is a jussive/subjunctive 'strive / try' addressed to the addressee. که here introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / that'. پیمان من = 'my covenant' (possessor after the noun), and نشکنی is the negative subjunctive of شکست‌ن meaning 'you do not break' (negation ن- + verb at the end).
segments.s297-g37: بدین کار = به + این + کار 'in this matter' (بدین is the fused instrumental 'by/with this' common in classical Persian). باشم is the first-person present/subjunctive 'I shall be / I will be' used here as a promise. ترا را = ترا (accusative/dative clitic before the noun for emphasis) plus the object marker را; رهنمای is the noun 'guide/adviser'. Word order is topical: instrument/location → verb → object → predicate noun.
segments.s297-g38: که introduces the result/purpose clause 'so that'. گنج و سپاهت = 'your treasure and your army' (possessive -ت attached to سپاه). بماند is the subjunctive/present 'remain / be left' placed at the end. به جای = 'in/at their place' (به + جای), giving the intended result.
segments.s297-g39: چو here functions as a conditional 'if'. کار سیاوش = 'the matter/case of Siyâvash' (possessive follows the noun). فرامش کنی is a light-noun construction where فرامش ('forgetfulness/neglect') takes کنی (کردن) to mean 'you forget / you neglect' and the verb comes at the end.
segments.s297-g40: نیارا is an adjectival/predicative word meaning 'unsuitable / incapable' and here is the predicate of the clause. به توران = 'for Turan' (direction/affected party) and به رامش = 'with/for tranquility/peace'; کنی (کردن) at the end means 'you make/bring about' — so the whole clause says 'you make Turan unsuitable for peace.'
segments.s297-g57: جز از + noun phrase expresses an exclusion ('except for ...'). Here جز از رزم و خون کردنت is an exclu­sive prepositional phrase ('except for fighting and causing your blood/shed­ding your blood') that serves as the complement to رای نیست — literally 'there is no counsel except ….' کردنت is the verbal noun (کردن + ـت) attached to خون, with کردن used causatively/lexically ('to cause / to make' someone bleed). رای نیست is a nominal predicate: رای ('counsel/plan') + نیست ('does not exist').
segments.s297-g58: به مغز تو ('in your mind') uses به + noun to mark location/mental sphere; مغز is 'mind/brain' and تو is the 2nd-person pronoun. پند مرا ('my advice') contains the possessive enclitic -م (here transcribed separately) + را (object/definiteness marker). جای نیست is an impersonal/existential phrase 'there is no place/room' — literally 'place is not (there)'; the whole clause means 'there is no room in your mind for my advice.'
segments.s297-g59: تو ... خرام is a present/jussive address to the second person: تو (you) + verbal form خرام ('stride/prance') — an archaic/poetic present form meaning 'you stride' or an imperative/encouragement 'stride!'. از لشکر خویش بیرون is a prepositional phrase 'out from your army/host', where لشکر خویش is 'your own host' (خویش expressed through خوی + enclitic ش). Word order is emphatic: pronoun — source — possessive — location — verb.
segments.s297-g60: مگر introduces an exception / conditional 'unless'. خود is emphatic 'yourself/on your own'. برآیدت is a classical/periphrastic verbal form (برآی- + personal enclitic -ت) meaning 'comes to you / becomes possible for you' (here: 'you can attain'). از این کار کام = 'satisfaction/desire from this deed' (از marks source or object of attainment). The clause reads 'unless you yourself can attain satisfaction from this deed.'
segments.s297-g61: بگردیم is 1st-person plural volitive/subjunctive ('let us go/let us turn') formed from گشتن/گردیدن; here it functions as an exhortation ('let us go'). هر دو ('both of us') follows the verb in this poetic order, and به آوردگاه ('to the battlefield') marks the destination. The construction is a classical, somewhat archaic word order (verb initially followed by subject phrase and direction).
segments.s297-g62: بر آساید is a predicate meaning 'be at peace / be relieved' (بر + آساید, a poetic/state form). از جنگ ('from war') marks what they are relieved of. چندین سپاه ('many a host/army') is the affected group. Put together the clause expresses purpose/result: if we go to the field, many hosts will be freed from warfare.
segments.s297-g63: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. من کشته آیم is a periphrastic passive/state: کشته (past participle 'killed') + آیم (present/subjunctive of آمدن used to form the resultant state) meaning 'when I am slain' or 'when I come to be slain'. جهان پیش تست is the main clause following the temporal clause: جهان ('the world') + پیش ('before/in front of') + تست (classical copula form meaning 'is yours' or 'is before you' in context).
segments.s297-g64: سپه بندگان و پسر خویش is a list of possessions: 'the army, the servants, and my own son' — خوی + ش is the possessive construction ('own/of me' given the speaker). تست again is the copula/concluding predicate ('will be yours' / 'are yours'). Grammatically, this continues the predication begun by جهان پیش تست: the things that will be 'before you' (i.e., yours) are enumerated.
segments.s297-g9: چنین بود رای جهان‌آفرین = literal order 'thus/so was the counsel of the World-Creator.' چَنین (thus) precedes the copula بود (was, past) for emphasis; رای (counsel/plan) is possessed by جهان‌آفرین ('the World‑Creator') and the whole phrase means 'such was the Creator's counsel.' Note that the genitive relation is expressed by juxtaposition (رای + جهان‌آفرین) as is common in poetic Persian.
segments.s297-g10: که introduces a content clause ('that ...') describing the content of that counsel. گردد is the third‑person singular of گَردیدن/گشتن used as 'become' (here future/subjunctive sense). جهان is the subject 'the world' and پر ز ... means 'full of ...' with ز = 'of/from' (poetic). پرخاش و کین is a coordinated noun phrase 'aggression/attack and hatred/vengeance.'
