segments.s297-g11: سیاوش نه بر بیگنه کشته شد = 'Siyavash was not killed by/from strangers.' نه negates the clause; بر + agent (بیگنه) marks the agent/party (here 'by/against strangers') and کشته شد is the passive past 'was killed' (کشته past participle + شد auxiliary). Word order places patient (سیاوش) first.
segments.s297-g12: شد از آموزگاران سرش گشته شد = a passive/result clause: شد here begins the clause ('it happened/it was') and از آموزگاران marks the agent/source 'by the teachers/advisors.' سرش is 'his head' (سر + enclitic ش 'his'), گشته is the past participle 'turned/been made,' and the final شد is the auxiliary forming the passive/result ('was turned/was made'). Together: 'it was by the teachers that his head was turned.'
segments.s297-g13: گنه‌گر مرا بود پیران چه کرد = word order is poetic/inverted: گنه‌گر 'guilty' is a predicate applied to 'مرا' (me, object marked here by the clitic and را), with بود as the past copula 'were/supposed to be.' پیران is the agent/subject 'the elders' and چه کرد is the question 'what did (they) do?' So overall: 'The elders held me to be guilty — what did they do?' Note را is the object-marking particle attached to the pronoun.
segments.s297-g14: چو رویین و لهاک و فرشیدورد = چو is a conjunctive particle here ('when' / 'as'), followed by a coordination of names/epithets (رویین, لهاک, فرشیدورد) joined by و. Grammatically this begins a temporal/subordinate phrase 'when Ruyin and Lahak and Farshidvard ...'.
segments.s297-g15: که بر پشت زینشان ببایست بست = subordinate clause introduced by که ('that/so that'). بر پشت زینشان = 'upon the backs of their saddles' (بر = on/upon; پشت = back; زینشان = their saddles). ببایست is the modal 'one must/ought to' and بست is the verb 'to bind/fasten' (perfective stem used as infinitival base here). Together: 'that (it) must be fastened on their saddles' backs.'
segments.s297-g16: پر از خون به کردار پیلان مست = descriptive phrase modifying what is fastened: پر از خون = 'full of blood' (پر 'full' + از 'of' + خون 'blood'). به کردار = 'in the manner/behavior of' (به used to form an adverbial phrase), پیلان = 'elephants' (plural of پیل), مست = 'drunk/intoxicated' (adjective). So: 'bloody, in the manner of drunken elephants.'
segments.s297-g65: وگر is the conditional 'if'. تو شوی کشته is a passive construction made with شوی (second-person present/subjunctive of شدن used here as auxiliary 'be') plus the past participle کشته → 'you be killed / if you are killed'. بر دست من is an agent/adjunct meaning 'by my hand' (preposition بر + noun + possessor). Word order places the conditional particle and subject before the passive predicate and its agent.
segments.s297-g66: کسی را is the direct object with the object marker را ('someone/anyone' as definite/accusative). نیازارم is a first-person finite verb phrase here (poetic/archaic surface form) functioning as the predicate 'I will not harm / I will not trouble' (the exact lexical senses in the supplied list do not match, so sense is marked -1). از انجمن links the object to its source/group with از + noun 'from the assembly'.
segments.s297-g67: سپاه تو is the subject 'your army'. در زینهار is an adverbial/locative phrase; زینهار here is used as a noun 'warning/admonition' or 'care/charge' and در marks location/'in/under'. منند (a poetic form of مانند/مانند) is a third-person plural predicate 'are like / stand as'. So literally 'Your army are in my zinehâr/are under my charge.'
segments.s297-g68: همه is a universal/topic particle 'all/everyone'. مهترانند is the third-person plural present of مهتر بودن 'are elders/chiefs'. و is the conjunction 'and'. یار منند repeats the same verb-form منند with یار as predicate noun 'companions/allies of mine' (literal: 'they are my yâr'). The clause uses parallel predicates linked by و.
segments.s297-g69: وگر زان که is a conditional/causal sequence ('and if / and if for that reason that'); زان که functions like 'that / because'. با من نیایی is a negative second-person present/subjunctive/imperative form meaning 'you do not come' or 'you do not join' (نیایی = 'do not come') with the preposition با marking accompaniment. به جنگ is the prepositional complement 'to war / to fight'.
segments.s297-g70: نتابی is the negative-form verb (ن + تابى) from تابیدن/تابیدن (here 'to endure, to bear') in second person: 'you will not endure/you cannot bear'. The pronoun تو can follow the verb for emphasis/inversion. با کار is an instrument/means phrase 'with the deed/act', and دیده نهنگ is a genitival/epithet 'the whale‑eyed' (دیده 'eye' + نهنگ 'whale' used as adjective), so the whole phrase means 'you will not withstand the deed/act of the whale‑eyed one.'
segments.s297-g71: کمر بسته is a circumstantial participial phrase 'girded at the waist / having girded his loins' (کمر = girdle/waist, بسته = bound/tied). پیش تو آید is a verb phrase 'comes before you' (پیش = before/in the presence of, تو = you, آید = he comes). پشنگ is the subject (a proper name) placed after the verb here for poetic word order.
segments.s297-g72: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if'. جنگ آوری is a verbal phrase 'you/he bring(s) war' (آوری from آوردن 'to bring' used to mean 'wage/bring into battle'); the subject is omitted and understood from context. او نسازد درنگ: او is 'he', نسازد is the negative present of ساختن 'to make/do' ('does not make/does not allow'), and درنگ is the object 'a pause/delay' — together 'he makes no delay' or 'he does not hesitate'.
segments.s297-g177: وگر is the conditional 'if' introducing a hypothetical. دور از ایدر is a prepositional phrase 'far from here' (دور + از + ایدر). تو گردی is a 2nd‑person present/subjunctive-like clause 'you become/you go' in an archaic poetic order (subject + verb), and هلاک is the predicate noun 'ruined/destroyed' describing the result.
segments.s297-g178: ز ایران برآید is a verb phrase 'will arise/come forth from Iran' (ز + place + verb برآید). یکی qualifies the following noun phrase as 'one' or 'a single', and تیره خاک is a noun phrase 'dark (tireh) soil/dust' functioning as the thing that 'arises.' The verb precedes the indefinite subject, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s297-g179: یکی زنده از ما نماند uses یکی in the sense 'not even one / no one' with a negative implication; زنده 'alive' modifies it. از ما 'of/from us' marks the group, and نماند is the past/present perfect sense 'remained/was left' (negated by the context). به جای is an adverbial phrase 'in place / remaining' completing the predicate.
segments.s297-g180: نه introduces a negation for the following list. شهر و بر و بوم ایران is a trifold enumeration 'cities and ... and homeland (land) of Iran' (بر و بوم is a fixed pairing meaning built places and land). به پای is idiomatic here, linked to نماند in the previous segment, meaning 'left standing' or 'remaining intact.' The sentence therefore enumerates what will not remain.
segments.s297-g181: کسی نیست is an existential negation 'there is no one.' ما را (lit. 'to/for us') and ز تخم کیان 'from the seed/lineage of Kian' specify whose absence is being stated: no one of our Kayanian stock. The word order places the negated existential first for emphasis.
segments.s297-g182: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. کین را is the object-marked noun 'vengeance' (را marks it definite/accusative). ببندد is a present/subjunctive-like verb 'would bind/gird' (archaic optative sense), and کمر here is the thing bound — 'waist / belt' used idiomatically for 'girding one's loins.' بر میان (literally 'on/for the middle') functions adverbially, completing the idiom of preparing for battle/vengeance.
segments.s297-g183: نیای تو is a possessive noun phrase 'your ancestor.' پیری جهاندیده is a nominal phrase: پیری (an old one/old age) modified by جهاندیده 'world‑wise/experienced.' است is the copula 'is.' The adjective/epithet follows the noun, giving the characterization 'an aged, worldly‑wise ancestor.'
segments.s297-g184: به توران و چین در پسندیده است is a locative/evaluative clause 'is esteemed/liked in Turan and China.' به marks the two regions as targets (توران و چین), در is the locative 'in', and پسندیده (a past participle used adjectivally) means 'pleasing/esteemed'; the copula است closes the clause. The ordering is poetically compact but reads as 'he is esteemed in Turan and China.'
segments.s297-g17: گر introduces a conditional ('if'); ایدونک is an emphatic/temporal particle meaning 'now' or 'at this moment'; گویم is the 1st‑person present ('I say') used to introduce the reported clause introduced by که; که here is the conjunction 'that'; تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun; بدتنی is a compound adjective 'bad‑bodied/ill‑favored' functioning as the predicate (the copula is omitted in Persian).
segments.s297-g18: بد اندیش is a compounded adjective meaning 'evil‑minded' (بد modifies اندیش); وز here functions as an archaic fused preposition 'و ز' = 'and from' (classical usage), linking the next noun phrase; تخم is 'seed' (here 'origin' or 'stock'); آهرمنی is an adjective derived from Ahriman meaning 'Ahrimanic' or 'demonic.'
segments.s297-g19: به گوهر نگه کن is an imperative clause: به marks the object/direction 'to/at', گوهر 'the jewel' (or essence), نگه 'a gaze/watch' plus the light verb کن (imperative 'do'), producing 'look at the jewel.' The following phrase به تخمه منم places the prepositional/possessive phrase 'of the seed' before منم 'I too / I am also', i.e. 'I too belong to that stock.'
segments.s297-g20: نکوهش (a noun 'reproach') combines with the light verb کنم ('I do') to form 'I reproach'; همی is the archaic continuous/habitual marker (modern Persian would omit it), marking the action as ongoing or habitual; خویشتن را — خویشتن 'self' with the object marker را makes 'myself' definite; کنَم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive of کردن used with the noun to express the action.
segments.s297-g21: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'; این کین is the noun phrase 'this grudge/hatred' (این specifying کین); به marks the direction/target 'against/toward' and introduces the persons named (گودرز و کاووس). مان here is an archaic element (likely a possessive/relational marker meaning 'of ours' or an emphatic particle) rather than the literal senses given in the lemma list.
segments.s297-g22: که introduces a relative clause 'who/which'; پیش من means 'before me / in my presence' (پیش = in front of); آرند is a 3rd‑person plural present ('they bring') used to describe the action of those named; لشکر دمان is a genitive phrase 'the army of Daman' or 'the army at the skirts' (دِمان here is locative/territorial), with دمان qualifying لشکر.
segments.s297-g23: نه zān گفتم این — here زان = 'for that reason / because of that' (classical use), and the negator نه negates the statement 'I said this for that reason.' گفتم is the 1st‑person past 'I said'; کز ('که از' contracted) introduces the subordinate clause 'that I was frightened by you'; ترسان شدم is a predicative structure meaning 'I became afraid' (ترسان as adjectival/participle, شدم = 'I became').
segments.s297-g24: وگر = 'and if / but if' (introduces the concessive clause); پیر گشتم 'I grew old' (پیر adjective + گشتم 'I became'); دگر سان شدم is an idiomatic predication 'I became of another sort' (دگر 'other' + سان 'like/as' + شدم 'I became') — the sequence shows change of state, with the second شدم as the copular verb.
segments.s297-g241: بدیشان (بد + ایشان) is an oblique pronoun phrase meaning 'to them' and stands before the object; چنین پاسخ is the object phrase 'this/such an answer.' آورد is the past tense verb 'brought/gave/uttered' used here to report speech, and شاه (the subject) comes after the verb in inverted/poetic word order.
segments.s297-g242: که introduces reported/direct speech ('that' / 'saying that'); ای (vocative) addresses the following موبدان 'O priests' (here written together as که‌ای). نماینده راه is an appositional noun phrase: 'representative' (نماینده) of the 'way' or 'path' (راه).
segments.s297-g243: بدانید is an imperative 'know/understand' (2nd person plural/formal). کاین is the fused conjunction + demonstrative ('that this'), introducing the subordinate clause. شیده is an adjectival past participle meaning 'mad/possessed' modifying روز 'day'; نبرد is the genitive/attributive noun 'of battle' (day of battle).
segments.s297-g244: پدر را is topicalized/definite 'the father' marked by the object marker را. ندارد is a negated present/past-tense verb 'does not have / there is not / will not remain' used impersonally here ('will not be left'). به هامون 'on the plain' and به مرد 'nor for any man' are prepositional phrases giving the locations/recipients; the doubleness of به lists two related complements (on the plain, and for man).
segments.s297-g245: سلیحش is سلیح + enclitic ش 'his weapon.' پدر کرده is a verbal phrase where پدر is the agent ('the father') and کرده is the past participle 'made' (so: 'his father had made'). از جادویی uses از to mark source/means: 'by/with magic' (جادویی = 'magical/sorcerous').
segments.s297-g246: ز is the poetic variant of از 'from' and introduces a list of qualities that characterize the magic: کژی 'crookedness/wrongness', بی راهی 'lack of the right path / waywardness' (بی + راهی), and بدخویی 'ill‑temper / bad disposition', coordinated by و.
segments.s297-g247: نباشد is the negative form of باشد here meaning 'will not be / is not'; it introduces a statement about the state of something. سلیح شما 'your weapon' (noun + possessive pronoun شما) is the subject/topic, and کارگر is an adjectival predicate meaning 'effective / operative / of use' positioned after the noun.
segments.s297-g248: بدان here is a fused prepositional particle (ب + ان/آن) functioning like 'against/versus that' (in context 'against that cuirass'); جوشن is 'cuirass/armor.' و links the next phrase; خود is emphatic 'himself,' and پولادبر is a compound meaning 'steel‑bearer' i.e. the man bearing steel (an armed/armored foe). Together they mark the targets against which the weapon will not be effective.

sentences.s297-s129: May I burn for him the dark life of his father; as Kavus burned for his son.
sentences.s297-s130: All the brave men and the lions of the land of Iran called out “Bravo” to the king.
sentences.s297-s101: The king kept his lips between his teeth and continually cast a fixed stare at them.
sentences.s297-s102: After that he said: 'This is not a way by which we can return to Iran from this battlefield.'
sentences.s297-s103: Where are all our customs and oaths — that same splendour, our words and counsel?
sentences.s297-s104: If Afrasiab remains alive on the throne, the world will become ruined because of him.
sentences.s297-s125: That very horse of his is descended from the wind; in heart like a lion and in its running like the wind.
sentences.s297-s126: Whoever God has not granted the farr to — he will not, even with his claw, have foot or feather.
sentences.s297-s127: That very one will not come to battle with you; from his farr and lineage shame will come upon him.
sentences.s297-s128: A scion of Fereydun and the son of Qobad — the two warriors were of one heart and one disposition.
sentences.s297-s97: Any city that lay beyond the border of Iran he would make empty of Turks.
sentences.s297-s98: We stride into Iran victorious and joyful; we do not take remembrance of deeds past.
sentences.s297-s99: Old and young were saying this, except for the renowned champion Rostam.
sentences.s297-s100: For Rostam, from reconciliation he turned away; because of Siavash's suffering he harbored hatred in his heart.
sentences.s297-s49: He chose four men from the mobeds, men who had experienced much of the world's warmth and cold.
sentences.s297-s50: And from those renowned men he (put together) an army of a thousand, wise and fit for battle.
sentences.s297-s51: As the vanguard was seen on the road from afar, he saw the banner and the spears of Turan's horsemen.
sentences.s297-s52: From the Turks: anyone who proved a poor vanguard was from the inexperienced, newly recruited youths.
sentences.s297-s113: Afrasiab the deceiver said that you hastened because of their fighting.
sentences.s297-s114: When the Iranians heard these words from the king, they were distressed/perturbed because of blame.
sentences.s297-s115: They begged pardon, saying, 'We are your servants; we are also abashed by your kindness.'
sentences.s297-s116: The shahanshah desires nothing but a good name; otherwise let his deeds have a good ending.
sentences.s297-s57: He said to the Iranians, "Send a horseman close to the king, with due ceremony and protocol."
sentences.s297-s58: He said that the bright‑hearted one called Shideh has brought several messages to the king.
sentences.s297-s59: From Afrasiab — that commander of Chin — (came) the parents of the king of the land of Iran.
sentences.s297-s60: A horseman (Doman) left the vanguard and proudly approached the king of Iran.
